data.title: EU wheat extends gains on weather concerns and Iran war worries
data.description:
European wheat futures rose to a one-month high on Wednesday, supported by dry weather in the U.S. wheat belt and soaring oil prices linked to the Strait of Hormuz standoff.

Benchmark September milling wheat (BL2U6) on Paris-based Euronext was up 1.4% at 217.25 euros ($254) a metric ton by 1515 GMT.

Chicago wheat was up 1%.

"Markets are supported by the dryness in the U.S. and high oil prices, both indirect but powerful factors," one German trader said. "Reports President Trump is planning a long-term blockade of the Hormuz Strait are the dominant factor in grain markets today."

European cash markets did not gain significant support from the dryness in the U.S., probably because Black Sea wheat was expected to dominate global exports in the new season, he said.

There were also hopes of more sales of EU wheat to U.S. East Coast buyers in the coming weeks if U.S. prices remain high.

Buyers in the United States purchased milling wheat in Poland to be supplied from the summer 2026 harvest because of high U.S. wheat prices, European traders said last week.

Export demand remained thin as buyers held back in the hope of an Iran peace deal.

Among thin demand for 11.5% protein wheat was a buyer in Libya seeking about 10,000 tons of EU wheat for May shipment and a Turkish buyer seeking about 10,000 tons at around $245 a ton cost and freight (c&f) for May shipment.

Indonesian buyers were seeking two panamax-size consignments of Bulgarian 11.5% wheat and two of Ukrainian 11.5% wheat for May/June shipment at $276 and $279 a ton c&f respectively.

An Egyptian buyer was also seeking 30,000 tons of Russian 12.5% wheat for May shipment at an undisclosed price while a Turkish importer sought 10,000 tons of Black Sea 12.5% wheat at $250 a ton c&f for May shipment.

data.title: Airbus Missed Earnings and the Stock Is Up. What That Could Mean for Boeing. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Airbus turned in mediocre first-quarter results, but the market didn't mind. The reaction is a good sign for aerospace investors looking for a recovery.

Tuesday evening, Airbus reported a quarterly operating profit of 300 million euros. Wall Street was looking for about EUR345 million.

A year ago, Airbus reported an operating profit of EUR624 million. Profits fell with deliveries. Airbus delivered 114 planes in the first quarter, down from 136 a year ago. That isn't a surprise. Airbus has been working through some quality issues and what the company calls a shortage of geared turbofan engines from RTX company Pratt & Whitney.

Both issues have been resolved, and Airbus left its full-year guidance unchanged. It still plans to deliver about 870 planes, up from 793 in 2025. It also still plans to generate and operate a profit of EUR7.5 billion, up from EUR7.1 billion in 2025.

Confidence is good. And it shows that airlines are still taking planes despite rising oil prices, which threaten their profits. It also shows that Airbus plans to build more planes. That might be even more important than oil prices.

Boeing and Airbus have chronically underproduced since the Covid-19 pandemic for several reasons, including internal issues at Boeing and supply-chain bottlenecks. It's taken a while to sort all that out. The pair is roughly 3,000 or 4,000 planes behind where the industry would like them to be.

There are roughly 30,000 commercial planes in service. The shortfall has kept older planes flying longer and theoretically kept airfares high, artificially capping demand. The exact impact is impossible to suss out, but the industry wants more planes right now.

Investors aren't so sure. Higher oil prices have shaken confidence. Since the start of the Iran war, the iShares Aerospace & Defense exchange-traded fund was down about 11% coming into Wednesday trading. Airbus shares were off 10%. GE Aerospace and RTX shares were down 16% and 13%, respectively. (Boeing is in the midst of a turnaround; its stock was up 1% since the start of fighting.)

Airbus shares were up 4.2% in overseas trading on Wednesday, which could signal better things to come for the industry and aerospace investors.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
